data.title: Bitcoin Around $90,000 As Ethereum, XRP, Dogecoin See Volatile Wednesday
data.description:
Bitcoin (CRYPTO: BTC) is back above $89,000 after a volatile trading day that saw the crypto king tap $88,000 and $90,000 in one session.

Notable Statistics:

  • Coinglass data shows 179,931 traders were liquidated in the past 24 hours for $807.30 million.       
  • In the past 24 hours, top gainers include Canton, MYX Finance and LayerZero.

Notable Developments:

  • Ripple President: Half Of Fortune 500 Will Have Crypto Strategies By Year-End
  • Bitcoin Down 11% After Trump’s First Year—Why Did BTC Perform Better Under Biden?
  • Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong Maintains $1 Million Per Bitcoin By 2030 Prediction
  • Bitcoin Will Catch Up To Gold Eventually, But Michael Saylor’s A Risk, Analysts Say
  • Bitcoin Bonuses: Steak ‘N Shake Announces Latest Perk For Hourly Workers
  • Bitcoin’s Fear And Greed Index Flashes Golden Cross: What Does It Mean?
  • Bitcoin Holders Have The Right Thesis But The Wrong Asset, Peter Schiff Says

Trader Notes: Trader Michael van de Poppe said Bitcoin is still holding a key support level after sweeping liquidity below recent lows.

With substantial liquidity resting lower and ample time remaining ahead of the Bank of Japan policy decision, he noted that a move toward $83,000 remains possible before any upside reversal.

CryptoCon observed that Bitcoin's Fear and Greed Index has shifted into "Fear," after spending December 2025 in "Extreme Fear."

The technical analyst cautioned that extreme fear does not automatically mark a buying opportunity, noting that after extended periods of greed, such shifts often signal a broader trend change.

The data shows that the market cycle appears complete, similar to prior cycles in which extreme greed was followed by high-timeframe bearish divergence.

Based on historical patterns, the current drawdown suggests the bear market may be approximately 20%–30% complete.

Analyst Kevin said Bitcoin is beginning to break down from a bear flag formation on the daily timeframe. He added that capital flows remain weak and do not yet support a sustained move higher.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Is Microsoft's AI Spending Paying Off?
data.description:
Microsoft Corp. (NASDAQ:MSFT) entered last earnings season carrying a heavy question on its back. After committing tens of billions of dollars to artificial intelligence infrastructure, investors wanted proof that the spending was beginning to translate into durable returns. The latest earnings print did not provide a simple answer, but it did give traders enough data to begin repricing expectations.

Microsoft delivered solid results across core segments, with Azure growth holding up better than feared and operating margins showing resilience despite elevated capital expenditures. The stock's reaction was measured rather than explosive, signaling a market that is no longer chasing the AI narrative blindly, but also not abandoning it.

For traders, the takeaway is less about whether Microsoft is winning the AI race and more about how efficiently it is converting that lead into earnings power.

Azure Growth Remains The Center Of The Debate

Azure remains Microsoft's most important growth engine and the focal point of investor scrutiny. Growth has moderated from the explosive pace seen during earlier cloud adoption cycles, but recent results suggest stabilization rather than continued deceleration.

That distinction matters. In the months leading into earnings, expectations had drifted lower as enterprises focused on cost optimization and delayed discretionary IT spending. Microsoft's ability to maintain steady Azure growth in that environment was viewed as a positive signal, even if it did not represent a sharp reacceleration.

For traders, Azure is increasingly being valued as a cash generating platform rather than a pure growth story. That shift places more emphasis on margin contribution and less on headline growth rates. As long as Azure avoids renewed slowing, the market appears willing to accept a more mature growth profile.

AI Infrastructure Spending And The Margin Question

The largest tension in Microsoft's story remains capital expenditures. AI infrastructure spending continues at a rapid pace, driven by data center expansion, specialized chips, and cloud capacity built to support generative AI workloads.

This level of investment naturally raises questions about margins. While Microsoft has a long history of disciplined capital allocation, the scale of current AI spending is unprecedented. Traders are now focused on whether incremental revenue from AI services can outpace the depreciation and operating costs tied to that infrastructure.

So far, margins have held up better than skeptics expected. Operating income growth suggests that Microsoft is absorbing higher costs without sacrificing profitability. That does not eliminate risk, but it does support the argument that Microsoft's scale and pricing power give it flexibility smaller competitors lack.

How The Market Is Interpreting The Results

Microsoft stock did not surge dramatically following earnings, nor did it sell off aggressively. That reaction itself is informative. The market appears to be shifting from a narrative-driven trade to an execution-driven one.

During earlier phases of the AI boom, any sign of leadership was enough to drive multiple expansion. Today, traders are more selective. They are watching margins, capex efficiency, and incremental revenue per dollar invested.

Microsoft's steady stock response suggests investors are comfortable with the current trajectory, but not yet convinced that AI monetization has reached an inflection point. In other words, optimism is being priced in gradually rather than all at once.

Comparing Microsoft's Position To Peers

Relative to other large technology companies, Microsoft occupies a unique position. Unlike firms that rely primarily on advertising or consumer hardware, Microsoft's AI exposure is embedded across enterprise software, cloud services, and productivity tools.

This diversification helps smooth volatility. While AI spending weighs on near term margins, Microsoft benefits from recurring revenue streams that can absorb investment cycles. Traders often view the company as both an AI leader and a defensive large cap, a combination that supports valuation during uncertain market conditions.

That positioning also means Microsoft does not need AI to succeed overnight. Gradual adoption and steady pricing improvements may be enough to justify continued investment.

Capex As A Signal, Not Just A Cost

One subtle shift in how traders are viewing Microsoft is the interpretation of capital expenditures. Rather than treating capex purely as a margin headwind, some are beginning to see it as a signal of confidence in future demand.

Microsoft is not investing blindly. Management has visibility into enterprise adoption trends and customer pipelines that the market does not fully see. Sustained capex at current levels suggests confidence that AI services will generate sufficient returns over time.

This does not guarantee success, but it changes the framing. Capex becomes a strategic bet rather than a red flag, provided revenue and margins remain resilient.

Technical And Sentiment Considerations

From a trading perspective, Microsoft's price action after its last reported earnings, reinforces its role as a market anchor. The stock continues to attract buyers on pullbacks, reflecting confidence in its long term earnings power.

At the same time, upside momentum has moderated compared to earlier AI driven rallies. That suggests traders are more inclined to wait for confirmation through future earnings rather than chase headlines.

Sentiment remains constructive but disciplined. That balance often precedes range bound trading rather than sharp directional moves.

Risks That Traders Cannot Ignore

Despite the constructive outlook, risks remain. If enterprise spending weakens materially, Azure growth could come under renewed pressure. If AI adoption fails to scale as expected, returns on infrastructure investment may lag.

There is also competitive risk. Rivals continue to invest aggressively, and pricing pressure could emerge if capacity outpaces demand across the industry.

Finally, broader market conditions matter. Microsoft's valuation still reflects premium expectations, making it sensitive to shifts in interest rates and risk appetite.

What Traders Should Watch Going Forward

For traders focused on Microsoft, several indicators stand out.

Azure growth trends will remain critical, particularly relative to peers. Capex levels should be monitored not just in absolute terms but relative to revenue growth. Operating margin stability will continue to act as a confidence check on AI spending.

Just as important will be management commentary. Subtle changes in tone around demand, utilization, and customer behavior often move the stock more than headline numbers.

A Stock Transitioning From Story To Scorecard

Microsoft's last reported earnings did not deliver a dramatic verdict on AI spending, but they did mark an important transition. The stock is no longer trading purely on promise. It is being evaluated on performance.

For traders, that shift creates a different opportunity set. Instead of betting on narrative momentum, positioning now revolves around execution, margins, and long term returns on capital.

AI spending may not be paying off all at once, but the early signs suggest Microsoft is laying the groundwork carefully. The market appears willing to give it time, as long as the numbers continue to support the story.

Benzinga Disclaimer: This article is from an unpaid external contributor. It does not represent Benzinga’s reporting and has not been edited for content or accuracy.

data.title: Markets Rally As Trump Drops Greenland's Tariff Threat: These 10 Stocks Are Soaring
data.description:
Risk-on sentiment snapped back Wednesday afternoon after President Donald Trump said planned tariffs on European Union countries will no longer take effect, citing progress on a Greenland framework with NATO leadership.

In a tweet on Truth Social, Trump said he will no longer impose the 10% tariffs on European Union countries that were scheduled to take effect on Feb. 1, following what he described as a "very productive" meeting with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte.

“We have formed the framework of a future deal with respect to Greenland and, in fact, the entire Arctic Region," Trump said.

"This solution, if consummated, will be a great one for the United States of America, and all NATO Nations."

Trump said negotiations will continue and extend beyond Greenland. "Additional discussions are being held concerning The Golden Dome as it pertains to Greenland," Trump said.

He said Vice President JD Vance, Secretary of State Marco Rubio, Special Envoy Steve Witkoff and others will lead negotiations.

Market Reactions

All major U.S. indices surged decisively higher, with participation expanding well beyond a narrow group of stocks.

The Russell 2000 climbed 2.0% to 2,697.66, leading the move as small-cap stocks reacted most directly to the easing of trade-related pressure.

The Nasdaq 100 – as tracked by the Invesco QQQ Trust (NASDAQ:QQQ) – followed closely, rising 1.9%.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average advanced 1.6% while the S&P 500 gained 1.6%.

More than $400 billion in market capitalization was added by Magnificent Seven stocks, led by gains in NVIDIA Corp. (NASDAQ:NVDA), Alphabet Inc. (NASDAQ:GOOG), Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL), Tesla Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA), and Meta Platforms Inc. (NASDAQ:META).

According to the Benzinga Pro platform, the strongest-performing stocks with market capitalizations above $50 billion during the last hour of trading included:

  • GE Vernova Inc. (NYSE:GEV): +3.20%
  • Novo Nordisk A/S (NYSE:NVO): +3.11%
  • Palantir Technologies Inc. (NYSE:PLTR): +2.92%
  • Vertiv Holdings Co. (NYSE:VRT): +2.64%
  • Advanced Micro Devices Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD): +2.34%
  • Cameco Corp. (NYSE:CCJ): +2.32%
  • Synopsys Inc. (NASDAQ:SNPS): +2.29%
  • Arm Holdings plc (NASDAQ:ARM): +2.17%
  • Cadence Design Systems Inc. (NASDAQ:CDNS): +2.13%
  • Marvell Technology Inc. (NASDAQ:MRVL): +2.11%

Photo: Shutterstock

data.title: Geopolitics, Volatility, Weak Dollar Fuel The 'Sell America' Trade— Global ETFs Can Be The Big Winners
data.description:
January's sharp rise in market volatility has forced investors to reassess how political risk is priced into portfolios. Since the start of 2026, the CBOE Volatility Index has surged more than 30%, underscoring how geopolitical tensions and policy uncertainty are increasingly influencing capital flows. In response, international equity ETFs are emerging as a practical hedge against U.S.-centric political shocks.

Volatility Turns Policy Risk Into A Portfolio Variable

Renewed transatlantic trade-war jazz, heightened geopolitical flashpoints, and public friction between President Donald Trump and Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell have amplified concerns about policy stability in the United States. Rather than exiting equities altogether, investors appear to be rotating geographically. According to LSEG Lipper data cited by Reuters, global equity funds attracted $45.59 billion in net inflows during the week ended Jan. 14, marking their strongest weekly inflow in nearly four months.

Global benchmarks reinforce this shift. The MSCI World Index has gained about 2.4% so far in 2026, while the S&P World Index has outperformed the S&P 500 over both the past year and year-to-date, highlighting growing confidence in non-U.S. equity exposure.

International Equity ETFs Offer Policy Diversification

Broad international equity ETFs are increasingly viewed as core allocations rather than tactical add-ons. Funds such as the Schwab International Equity ETF (NYSE:SCHF), Dimensional International Core Equity Market ETF (NYSE:DFAI), Avantis International Equity ETF (NYSE:AVDE), and Schwab Fundamental International Equity ETF (NYSE:FNDF) provide diversified exposure across developed markets, helping investors dilute concentrated U.S. policy risk while maintaining equity exposure.

These ETFs benefit from steadier policy environments and a weakening U.S. dollar, which has added a currency tailwind to international returns amid expectations of further Federal Reserve rate cuts in 2026.

Dividend Strategies Cushion Volatility

Dividend-focused international ETFs are also drawing attention as volatility buffers. The WisdomTree International Hedged Quality Dividend Growth Fund (NYSE:IHDG) and iShares International Select Dividend ETF (BATS:IDV) offer income-oriented exposure that can help stabilize returns during periods of heightened market uncertainty.

Dividend-paying companies often display stronger balance sheets, making these funds particularly attractive when political and macro risks dominate headlines.

Emerging Markets Add Growth Optionality

For investors willing to assume additional risk, emerging market ETFs offer exposure to faster-growing economies less directly tied to U.S. policy dynamics. With the Dow Jones Emerging Markets Index up 27% over the past year, funds such as the iShares Core MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (NYSE:IEMG), Vanguard FTSE Emerging Markets ETF (NYSE:VWO), and iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (NYSE:EEM) are gaining traction.

As political uncertainty persists and global growth expectations stabilize, global ETFs are increasingly serving as diversification tools as well as strategic instruments for managing political risk.

Image created using artificial intelligence via Midjourney.

data.title: Oklo Stock Rises As Trump Backs Nuclear Energy
data.description:
Oklo Inc (NYSE:OKLO) shares are trading higher alongside other uranium and nuclear‑linked names after President Donald Trump signaled strong support for nuclear power at the Davos meeting, saying the U.S. is "very much into the world of nuclear energy."

Trump’s statement is positioning nuclear power as a clean and reliable alternative to what he called costly and inconsistent wind and solar options. His remarks follow recent executive actions aimed at speeding up reactor licensing and accelerating deployment of small modular reactors.

"The USA is the economic engine on the planet," Trump stated. "You all follow us down, and you follow us up."

Meta’s Investment Signals Nuclear Energy’s Future

Additionally, Bank of America analyst Dimple Gosai upgraded from Neutral to Buy and raised the price target to $127 from $111, pointing to the company's newly signed binding agreement with Meta Platforms Inc (NASDAQ:META) as a major milestone for advanced nuclear development.

The upgrade comes after Oklo announced a firm, multi‑phase deal with Meta to build an approximately 1.2 GW advanced nuclear campus in Pike County, Ohio. As part of the agreement, Meta will provide $25 million in Phase 1 prepayments to support early development work ahead of final power purchase agreements.

BofA called the Meta partnership "tangible proof of execution," saying it shows advanced nuclear is moving from theory to real‑world deployment.

BofA's higher $127 price target reflects expanding peer multiples, which is now 25x/19x on 2032/2033 estimates versus 16.5x/15.6x previously, as well as added revenue tied to the Meta deal. The firm's 2036 revenue estimate rises to $5.9 billion from $5.5 billion, with projected deployed capacity increasing to 6.7 GW from 6.3 GW.

OKLO Price Action: Oklo shares were flat at 0.69% at $90.53 at the time of publication on Wednesday, according to Benzinga Pro.

Image: Piotr Swat/Shutterstock
